original_abstract: Chlorophylls are important antioxidants found in foods. We explored the mechanisms through which the a and b forms of chlorophyll and of pheophytin (the Mg-chelated form of chlorophyll) reduce oxidation: we used comet assay to measure prevention of H2O2 DNA damage; we tested for quenching of 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH); we measured the ability to chelate Fe(II); and, we tested their ability to prevent formation of thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) during Cu-mediated peroxidation of low density lipoprotein (LDL) in a chemical assay. All chlorophylls and pheophytins showed significant dose-dependent activity in the assays, with the pheophytins being the strongest antioxidants. Thus, these chemicals can prevent oxidative DNA damage and lipid peroxidation both by reducing reactive oxygen species, such as DPPH, and by chelation of metal ions, such as Fe(II), which can form reactive oxygen species.
humanized_abstract: Objectives: Chlorophylls are antioxidants that are present in plant derived foods. Their role in protecting cells and organisms from damage caused by free radicals and reactive oxygen species is poorly studied, especially with regard to biological assays. Our objective was to assess the antioxidant activity of chlorophyll a and b as well as their Mg-free derivatives, pheophytin a and b and thus to assess the role of the magnesium in their antioxidant properties. We have used the comet assay, (assessing DNA damage by H2O2), the DPPH assay (assessing scavenging activity), iron chelation (assessing chelation of Fe (II) ions), as well as a biochemical assay for LDL oxidation (assessing inhibition of TBARS formation) during Cu-induced peroxidation. Results: Chlorophyll a and b as well as pheophytin a and b showed a dose-dependent antioxidant activity in all the tests and more so with pheophytins suggesting that the magnesium is not crucial for their antioxidant activity and can be even harmful to some extent. Therefore, chlorophylls and pheophytins act as strong antioxidants capable of preventing oxidative DNA damage and lipid peroxidation by scavenging ROS as well as through the chelation of metal ions that are involved in the production of ROS.
